intro: It rarely makes the front page, because progress is slow and bad news is loud. But step back far enough and the trend is unmistakable: across the long arc, humanity is getting healthier, safer, more literate and less poor. Here's the proof — in the numbers.

metrics.title: Extreme poverty
metrics.blurb: Share of the world living in extreme poverty. Two centuries ago it was almost everyone; today it's fewer than one in ten.

metrics.title: Child mortality
metrics.blurb: Share of children who die before their fifth birthday. In 1800 it was nearly half of all children; it has fallen further in every decade since.

metrics.title: Life expectancy
metrics.blurb: How long a newborn can expect to live, worldwide. It has more than doubled since 1800 — the average person now lives into their seventies.

metrics.title: Literacy
metrics.blurb: Share of adults who can read and write. Two centuries ago only 1 in 10 could; today nearly 9 in 10 can — more than five billion people.

metrics.title: Child deaths per year
metrics.blurb: The number of children under five who die each year — most from preventable causes. It has fallen by well over half since 1990.

metrics.title: Homicide in Western Europe
metrics.blurb: Murders per 100,000 people. Over seven centuries, lethal violence in everyday life has collapsed to a tiny fraction of what it once was.
